content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 23 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 111r–112v | **Source Images:** DEU_023_page_001.jpg, DEU_023_page_002.jpg, DEU_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** "No man who has been emasculated by crushing or cutting may enter the assembly of the LORD.

**2** "No one born of a forbidden union may enter the assembly of the LORD, not even his descendants — down to the tenth generation, none of them may enter the assembly of the LORD.

**3** "No Ammonite or Moabite may enter the assembly of the LORD. Even to the tenth generation, none of their descendants may ever enter the assembly of the LORD, **4** because they did not come to meet you with bread and water on your journey out of Egypt, and because they hired Balaam son of Beor from Pethor in Aram-Naharaim to pronounce a curse on you. **5** But the LORD your God refused to listen to Balaam. He turned the curse into a blessing for you, because the LORD your God loves you. **6** Do not seek peace or good relations with them as long as you live.

**7** "Do not despise an Edomite, for the Edomites are your relatives. Do not despise an Egyptian, because you lived as a foreigner in their land. **8** The third generation of children born to them may enter the assembly of the LORD.

**9** "When you are encamped against your enemies, keep yourself from anything impure.

**10** "If one of your men is ceremonially unclean because of a nocturnal emission, he must go outside the camp and stay there. **11** But as evening approaches, he must wash himself, and at sunset he may return to the camp.

**12** "Designate a place outside the camp where you can go to relieve yourself. **13** As part of your equipment have something to dig with, and when you relieve yourself, dig a hole and cover up your excrement. **14** For the LORD your God moves about in your camp to protect you and to hand your enemies over to you. Your camp must be holy, so that he will not see anything indecent among you and turn away from you.

**15** "If a slave has escaped from his master and comes to you, do not hand him over to his master. **16** Let him live among you wherever he chooses, in whatever town suits him best. Do not oppress him.

**17** "No Israelite man or woman is to become a temple prostitute. **18** You must not bring the earnings of a female prostitute or the wages of a male prostitute into the house of the LORD your God to pay any vow, because the LORD your God detests them both.

**19** "Do not charge a fellow Israelite interest — whether on money, food, or anything else that earns interest. **20** You may charge a foreigner interest, but not a fellow Israelite, so that the LORD your God may bless you in everything you do in the land you are about to enter and possess.

**21** "If you make a vow to the LORD your God, do not delay in fulfilling it, for the LORD your God will certainly demand it of you, and you will be guilty of a sin. **22** But if you choose not to make a vow, you will not be guilty of any sin. **23** Whatever you say with your lips you must be sure to do, because you made the vow voluntarily to the LORD your God with your own mouth.

**24** "If you enter your neighbor's vineyard, you may eat all the grapes you want until you are satisfied, but do not put any in your basket. **25** If you enter your neighbor's grain field, you may pick heads of grain with your hands, but you must not put a sickle to your neighbor's standing grain.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The assembly of the LORD (*qahal YHWH*) (vv. 1–8):** The *qahal* is Israel gathered for worship and communal covenant life — specifically at the sanctuary. The restrictions in these verses are not about personal salvation but about participation in the formal covenant assembly. The categories are: physical incompleteness (v. 1), illegitimate birth (v. 2), hostile foreigners (vv. 3–6), and partially acceptable foreigners (vv. 7–8). PNEUMA renders "assembly of the LORD" throughout for consistency rather than "congregation" or "community."

**Ammon and Moab excluded permanently (vv. 3–6):** The reason is hospitality violation and the attempt to curse Israel (through Balaam). The LORD's reversal of the curse into blessing (v. 5) is a crucial piece of the story: the restriction on Ammon and Moab is not merely ethnic but rooted in their historical hostility at a critical moment. The story of Ruth the Moabite (set in the same period as Judges) creates a deliberate tension with this law — the spirit of the person overriding ethnic status.

**"The LORD your God loves you" (v. 5):** *Ki ahavcha YHWH Elohecha* — a rare explicit statement of divine love as the motivation for a specific protective act. PNEUMA renders this as a full sentence, giving it the weight it deserves.

**Edomites and Egyptians: partial acceptance (vv. 7–8):** Edom is "your brother" (*achikha*) — Jacob and Esau were brothers. Egypt housed Israel but also enslaved them. Neither gets immediate full acceptance, but the third generation may enter the assembly. The logic moves from enmity to gradual integration.

**Camp purity (vv. 9–14):** The laws about nocturnal emissions and waste management may appear mundane, but the theological rationale is explicit: "the LORD your God moves about in your camp" (v. 14). Divine presence requires holiness. The camp is sacred space. PNEUMA renders *yadkha* in v. 13 as "as part of your equipment" — literally "on your equipment/weapon-peg" — capturing the military context.

**Escaped slaves (vv. 15–16):** This law stands in marked contrast to most ancient Near Eastern law codes, which required the return of escaped slaves and punished those who harbored them. Israel's law forbids extradition and mandates free settlement. PNEUMA renders "wherever he chooses" as the full scope of freedom offered: *baqqomer tov lo* means "in the best place for him."

**Temple prostitution (v. 17):** *Qedeshah* and *qadesh* — literally "consecrated woman/man" — are here used for cult prostitutes associated with Canaanite fertility worship. PNEUMA renders "temple prostitute" for clarity in this specific prohibitory context, where the connection to idolatrous practice is the point.

**Interest-free loans to Israelites (vv. 19–20):** *Neshekh* (interest, from the root "to bite") is prohibited between Israelites. The economic protection is communal: lending at interest to a desperate neighbor compounds their misery. Foreigners — engaged in commerce rather than survival situations — may be charged interest. PNEUMA renders this economic distinction without judgment on the foreigner-distinction, which the text ties directly to covenantal blessing (v. 20).

**Vows (vv. 21–23):** The principle is: better not to vow than to vow and fail to deliver. *Tichadel lindar* — "if you refrain from making vows" — is not a prohibition of vows but a frank acknowledgment that delay or failure has moral weight. PNEUMA renders v. 23 with emphasis on the voluntary nature: "you made the vow voluntarily... with your own mouth."

**Neighbor's produce (vv. 24–25):** The right to eat while passing through — but not to harvest for later. A sickle in the grain field is commercial taking; hands-only is personal sustenance. This law protects both the traveler and the landowner, and becomes the legal basis for the Pharisees' challenge to Jesus' disciples in the Gospels.

*— PNEUMA*
